food_name: Rojak (Singapore fruit and vegetable salad with prawn paste sauce)
food_subgroup: Fresh fruit and vegetable salad with prawn paste dressing

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Singapore Rojak. Closest parent is fruit-vegetable salad with fermented shrimp paste dressing. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fruit and vegetable salad with fermented black prawn paste dressing and peanuts (Singapore rojak style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0SAL is the closest FoodEx2 parent for mixed salad dishes; no Singapore-specific rojak code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Singapore Rojak as a specific food item.

preparation_method: dressed with fermented prawn paste sauce

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of Singapore Rojak weighs approximately 300g, comprising a mix of fresh fruits, vegetables, fried dough fritters, and prawn paste dressing topped with crushed peanuts.

nutrient_source.title: Rojak (Singapore Malay style) — HPB Singapore Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Singapore Malay-style Rojak. HPB Foodo is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — University of Sydney GI Database; GI driven by fresh fruit components (pineapple GI ~59, mango GI ~51–60) and added sugar in hae ko dressing
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore Rojak as a composite dish. GI of 60 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database values for the primary carbohydrate contributors: fresh pineapple (GI ~59), green mango (GI ~51–60), and added sugar in the hae ko dressing. Dietary fibre from fruit components (1.8g/100g) and acidity from lime juice provide modest glycaemic moderation. GL per 300g plate = (60 × 17.5 × 3) / 100 = 31.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium from fermented black prawn paste (hae ko) dressing — 485mg sodium per 100g; a standard 300g plate delivers approximately 1,455mg sodium, representing approximately 60% of the 2,400mg daily recommended limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request the hae ko dressing served on the side and use it sparingly, or avoid rojak entirely on high-sodium days; the sweetness of the dressing masks its saltiness, making it easy to underestimate sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate-high sugar content (9.5g/100g) from fresh fruit and added sugar in the hae ko dressing, with a medium GI of 60 and a glycaemic load of approximately 31.5 per 300g plate.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150g or less, request reduced dressing, and pair rojak with a low-GI meal component to moderate the overall glycaemic response; the dietary fibre (1.8g/100g) and lime juice acidity provide partial glycaemic moderation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content (485mg/100g) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low (0.8g/100g) and trans fat is negligible (0.0g/100g), while vitamin C (22mg/100g) and antioxidant-rich fruits provide cardioprotective micronutrients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should treat rojak as an occasional food due to its high sodium load from the prawn paste dressing, while benefiting from its low saturated fat profile and antioxidant fruit content when consumed in a reduced-dressing portion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C content (22mg/100g) from pineapple, guava, and lime juice; a 300g plate provides approximately 66mg vitamin C (73% of the 90mg/day RDA), supporting antioxidant defence in joint tissues.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Rojak is a suitable occasional food for people with rheumatoid arthritis due to its antioxidant-rich fruit base and low saturated fat; sodium from the dressing should still be monitored if corticosteroids are in use.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Singapore Rojak is a distinctive fruit and vegetable salad that embodies the city-state's multicultural hawker food identity. The dish is built on a base of fresh tropical fruits and vegetables — typically pineapple, water guava (jambu air), green mango, jicama (sengkuang), cucumber, bean sprouts, and kangkung — tossed together with crispy yu char kway (Chinese fried dough fritters) and coated in a thick, jet-black, intensely flavoured dressing made from hae ko (fermented black prawn paste), sugar, chili, and lime juice, then finished with a generous topping of crushed peanuts and toasted sesame seeds. The name 'rojak' literally means 'mixture' in Malay, and the dish is a culinary metaphor for Singapore's mixed cultural heritage. The flavour profile is a striking combination of sweet, sour, salty, spicy, and umami — unlike any other salad in world cuisine. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 118 kcal, 3.2g protein, 4.5g fat (0.8g saturated), 17.5g carbohydrate, 9.5g sugar, 1.8g dietary fibre, and 485mg sodium. The very high sodium content (485mg/100g; ~1,455mg per 300g plate) is the primary clinical concern, driven entirely by the hae ko dressing, making it unsuitable for hypertension patients without significant dressing reduction. The medium GI (~60) and moderate glycaemic load (~31.5 per plate) require portion control for people with Type 2 Diabetes. On the positive side, the dish is rich in vitamin C (22mg/100g), vitamin A (48mcg RAE/100g), potassium (245mg/100g), and folate (28mcg/100g), reflecting its fresh fruit and vegetable base. Rojak is sold at hawker centres, food courts, and Malay-Chinese food stalls throughout Singapore, typically as a snack or starter.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved source for Singapore dishes under Project Veritas v2 source policy. The HPB Foodo entry for Singapore Malay-style Rojak is an exact match for this dish. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values, clearly documented. All mandatory multilingual names are present. Core macros are complete and internally consistent.
notes: Trans fat is reported as 0.0g/100g — confirmed negligible/trace per HPB Foodo source for this dish; this is consistent with a fresh fruit and vegetable salad with minimal processed fat. Vitamin D is reported as 0.0mcg/100g — confirmed zero as this dish contains no significant vitamin D sources (no oily fish, eggs, or fortified dairy). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdowns are not reported by HPB Foodo for this entry and are set to -1.0. Water content is not reported by the source and is set to -1.0. The two portions in the input (300g and 150g) are preserved; 300g is the standard hawker plate and is used as the default serving for GL calculation. The input had two entries with is_default: true in portions — corrected to use 300g as the single default serving for GL purposes. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate; a direct measurement for Singapore Rojak does not exist in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date.

improvement_actions_taken: Mapped input per_100g field names to RESULT template field names (e.g. energy → energy_kcal, fat → fat_g, etc.).
Added mandatory Hindi local name (रोजक) absent from input.
Restructured health_suitability into health_context with required condition keys (hypertension, type2_diabetes, cardiovascular_disease) and added cardiovascular_disease entry not present in input.
Renamed diabetes condition key to type2_diabetes with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per scoring model requirement.
Added concern_level and key_factor fields to each health_context entry.
Converted flat alias objects (with alias/language/alias_type/is_primary fields) to flat string aliases list and populated aliases_by_language object.
Ensured all aliases_by_language string values appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Added foodex2_gap_note and snomed_ct_gap_note fields to ontology section.
Set trans_fat_g to 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg to 0.0 with justification in notes (confirmed negligible/zero per source).
Set monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and water_g to -1.0 as not reported by source.
Expanded llm_training.natural_language_description to exceed 200 characters with full clinical and cultural context.
Corrected dual is_default:true portions issue — documented in notes, used 300g as GL calculation basis.
